answer:
Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Rate Limiting covers algorithm trade-offs (token bucket, sliding window, leaky bucket, fixed window counter), distributed rate limiting with Redis, failure modes (clock skew, race conditions, bypass via IP rotation), and rollback plans when rate limiting misconfiguration blocks legitimate traffic.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented rate limiting at two layers: per-user API limits (100 req/min for normal users, 500 for managers) and per-endpoint limits (leave submission capped at 10/min to prevent accidental duplicates). During the annual leave flush period, the system needed to handle legitimate bursts from HR admins bulk-processing 200+ leave adjustments while still blocking abuse.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Distributed sliding window rate limiter with Redis
import { createClient } from 'redis';
const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });

interface RateLimitConfig {
  windowMs: number;
  maxRequests: number;
}

const TIER_LIMITS: Record = {
  employee: { windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 100 },
  manager:  { windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 500 },
  admin:    { windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 2000 },
};

async function slidingWindowCheck(userId: string, tier: string): Promise<{ allowed: boolean; remaining: number; retryAfter?: number }> {
  const config = TIER_LIMITS[tier] || TIER_LIMITS.employee;
  const key = `ratelimit:${userId}`;
  const now = Date.now();
  const windowStart = now - config.windowMs;

  // Atomic sliding window using Redis sorted set
  const multi = redis.multi();
  multi.zRemRangeByScore(key, 0, windowStart); // Remove expired entries
  multi.zCard(key);                            // Count remaining
  multi.zAdd(key, { score: now, value: `${now}:${Math.random()}` });
  multi.expire(key, Math.ceil(config.windowMs / 1000));

  const results = await multi.exec();
  const currentCount = results[1] as number;

  if (currentCount >= config.maxRequests) {
    const oldest = await redis.zRangeWithScores(key, 0, 0);
    const retryAfter = oldest[0] ? Math.ceil((oldest[0].score + config.windowMs - now) / 1000) : 60;
    return { allowed: false, remaining: 0, retryAfter };
  }

  return { allowed: true, remaining: config.maxRequests - currentCount - 1 };
}

// Fastify plugin
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const userId = req.user?.id || req.ip;
  const tier = req.user?.role || 'employee';
  const result = await slidingWindowCheck(userId, tier);

  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Limit', TIER_LIMITS[tier].maxRequests);
  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', result.remaining);

  if (!result.allowed) {
    reply.header('Retry-After', result.retryAfter);
    reply.code(429).send({ error: 'Rate limit exceeded', retryAfter: result.retryAfter });
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: HR admin reported getting 429 errors while bulk-processing year-end leave adjustments (200 updates in 3 minutes). Meanwhile, a script was hammering the leave API from an external IP without being rate-limited.

Investigation: Manoj checked the rate limiter config — admin tier was set to 100 req/min (same as employee, copy-paste bug). The external script bypassed rate limiting because it hit a health-check endpoint that was excluded from the rate limiter but also allowed proxied requests to the main API through a misconfigured nginx rule.

Fix: (1) Fixed admin tier limit to 2000 req/min. (2) Added rate limiting to ALL endpoints including health checks. (3) Added IP-based rate limiting (10K req/min per IP) as a second layer. (4) Made rate limits configurable via Redis keys so they can be adjusted without deploys.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Fixed window boundary burst (2× allowed requests)Use sliding window log or sliding window counter hybrid
Distributed clock skew across nodesUse Redis server time (TIME command) instead of local clock
Rate limiter blocks legitimate admin operationsRole-based tiers with configurable limits via Redis keys
Bypass via IP rotation or distributed botsCombine user-level + IP-level + endpoint-level limits

Validation Strategy

  • Boundary test: send requests at window boundary (last second + first second), verify no 2× burst
  • Tier test: verify employee capped at 100/min, manager at 500/min, admin at 2000/min
  • Distributed test: send requests through 3 different gateway instances, verify global count is correct
  • Rollback test: update Redis config key to 10× limits, verify takes effect within 1 second

Definition

Architecting Rate Limiting for 1M+ users requires a distributed, multi-layer throttling system: edge-level (CDN/WAF) for DDoS protection, application-level for business logic limits, and per-tenant quotas for fair resource allocation. The system must handle 100K+ rate limit checks per second with sub-millisecond overhead.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub, 1M users perform contact CRUD and CSV bulk imports. Manoj would architect: (1) Cloudflare edge rate limiting at 1000 req/min per IP, (2) Redis-based sliding window at the Fastify layer — free tier: 50 req/min, premium: 500 req/min, (3) per-operation limits (CSV import: 3 per hour, search: 200 per minute). The challenge: bulk CSV import (500 contacts/batch) counts as 1 request but generates 500 DB writes.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Multi-layer rate limiting system
const RATE_CONFIGS = {
  global: { default: { windowMs: 60_000, max: 50 }, premium: { windowMs: 60_000, max: 500 } },
  endpoints: {
    'POST:/api/contacts/import':  { windowMs: 3_600_000, max: 3 },
    'GET:/api/contacts/search':   { windowMs: 60_000, max: 200 },
    'POST:/api/contacts':         { windowMs: 60_000, max: 30 },
  },
  costGuards: {
    dbWrites:    { windowMs: 3_600_000, max: 5000 },
    cloudinary:  { windowMs: 3_600_000, max: 100 },
  },
};

// Token bucket with local cache for hot users
const localBuckets = new Map();

function localTokenCheck(userId: string, maxTokens: number, refillRate: number): boolean {
  const now = Date.now();
  let bucket = localBuckets.get(userId);
  if (!bucket) {
    bucket = { tokens: maxTokens, lastRefill: now };
    localBuckets.set(userId, bucket);
  }
  const elapsed = (now - bucket.lastRefill) / 1000;
  bucket.tokens = Math.min(maxTokens, bucket.tokens + elapsed * refillRate);
  bucket.lastRefill = now;
  if (bucket.tokens >= 1) {
    bucket.tokens -= 1;
    return true; // Skip Redis check
  }
  return false; // Fall through to Redis for precise check
}

// Fastify rate limiting hook
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const userId = req.user?.id || req.ip;
  const tier = req.user?.tier || 'default';

  // Layer 1: Local token bucket (sub-microsecond)
  const globalConfig = RATE_CONFIGS.global[tier];
  if (localTokenCheck(userId, globalConfig.max, globalConfig.max / 60)) {
    return; // Fast path: user has plenty of budget
  }

  // Layer 2: Redis sliding window (authoritative)
  const result = await slidingWindowCheck(userId, tier);
  if (!result.allowed) {
    reply.code(429).header('Retry-After', result.retryAfter)
      .send({ error: 'Rate limit exceeded', tier, retryAfter: result.retryAfter });
    return;
  }

  // Layer 3: Per-endpoint limit
  const endpointKey = `${req.method}:${req.routeOptions.url}`;
  const endpointConfig = RATE_CONFIGS.endpoints[endpointKey];
  if (endpointConfig) {
    const epResult = await slidingWindowCheck(`${userId}:${endpointKey}`, tier, endpointConfig);
    if (!epResult.allowed) {
      reply.code(429).send({ error: `Endpoint rate limit exceeded for ${endpointKey}` });
    }
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: Free-tier ContactsHub users discovered they could bypass the 3-per-hour CSV import limit by creating multiple accounts with different emails. Meanwhile, premium users were occasionally rate-limited during legitimate usage spikes.

Investigation: Manoj found that rate limiting was purely per-userId. A single person with 5 free accounts could do 15 imports/hour. Premium users were hitting endpoint-specific limits that were set to the same values as free tier (copy-paste from config).

Fix: (1) Added IP-based rate limiting as a secondary layer (max 5 imports/hour per IP regardless of accounts). (2) Separated endpoint limits by tier. (3) Added a cost-guard layer that tracked actual DB write operations — each CSV import of 500 contacts counted as 500 writes against a 5000/hour budget. (4) Added Cloudinary operation limits to prevent image processing abuse.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Single-layer limiting easily bypassedMulti-layer: IP + user + endpoint + cost-based limits
Redis becomes bottleneck for rate checksLocal token bucket for fast path; Redis only for precise enforcement
Legitimate burst traffic gets blockedToken bucket allows configurable burst size above sustained rate
Configuration errors block all trafficRedis-based config with admin override endpoint; auto-relax on >10% 429 rate

Validation Strategy

  • Multi-account test: create 5 accounts from same IP, verify combined import limit enforced
  • Tier isolation: verify free user at 50 req/min and premium at 500 req/min simultaneously
  • Redis failure test: kill Redis, verify local token bucket provides degraded rate limiting
  • Latency test: measure rate check overhead, verify < 1ms p99 under 100K checks/s

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Event-Driven Design examines when to use events vs. direct calls, event schema evolution, ordering guarantees, exactly-once vs. at-least-once delivery, saga patterns for distributed transactions, failure modes (poison messages, event storms, consumer lag), and rollback strategies for event-driven systems.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj would refactor the leave approval flow from synchronous REST calls to an event-driven architecture. When a leave is approved: (1) LeaveApproved event published to Redis Streams, (2) notification-service consumes it and sends Socket.io + email, (3) calendar-service updates team calendar, (4) balance-service decrements days. If any consumer fails, the event remains in the stream for retry — no data loss.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Event publisher with Redis Streams
import { createClient } from 'redis';
const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });

interface LeaveApprovedEvent {
  type: 'LeaveApproved';
  version: 2;
  data: {
    leaveId: string;
    employeeId: string;
    approverId: string;
    startDate: string;
    endDate: string;
    days: number;
    departmentId: string;
  };
  metadata: { correlationId: string; timestamp: number; source: string };
}

async function publishLeaveApproved(leave: any, approverId: string) {
  const event: LeaveApprovedEvent = {
    type: 'LeaveApproved',
    version: 2,
    data: {
      leaveId: leave.id,
      employeeId: leave.employeeId,
      approverId,
      startDate: leave.startDate.toISOString(),
      endDate: leave.endDate.toISOString(),
      days: leave.days,
      departmentId: leave.departmentId,
    },
    metadata: {
      correlationId: crypto.randomUUID(),
      timestamp: Date.now(),
      source: 'approval-service',
    },
  };

  await redis.xAdd('leave-events', '*', {
    event: JSON.stringify(event),
  });
}

// Consumer with consumer group (at-least-once delivery)
async function startNotificationConsumer() {
  // Create consumer group (idempotent)
  try {
    await redis.xGroupCreate('leave-events', 'notification-group', '0', { MKSTREAM: true });
  } catch { /* group exists */ }

  while (true) {
    const messages = await redis.xReadGroup('notification-group', 'consumer-1', [
      { key: 'leave-events', id: '>' },
    ], { COUNT: 10, BLOCK: 5000 });

    if (!messages) continue;

    for (const msg of messages[0].messages) {
      const event = JSON.parse(msg.message.event) as LeaveApprovedEvent;
      try {
        // Idempotency check
        const processed = await redis.sIsMember('processed-events', msg.id);
        if (processed) { await redis.xAck('leave-events', 'notification-group', msg.id); continue; }

        await sendNotification(event.data.employeeId, `Leave approved by manager`);
        await redis.sAdd('processed-events', msg.id);
        await redis.xAck('leave-events', 'notification-group', msg.id);
      } catch (err) {
        console.error('Failed to process event', msg.id, err);
        // Don't ACK — message will be redelivered after visibility timeout
      }
    }
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After switching leave approval to event-driven, employees reported receiving duplicate notification emails — some got 5 copies of "Your leave has been approved."

Investigation: Manoj checked the consumer logs. The notification consumer was processing events but crashing after sending the email (before ACKing the message). On restart, the same events were redelivered. The consumer had no idempotency check — it just sent emails for every event it received.

Fix: Added an idempotency layer using a Redis Set of processed message IDs. Before processing an event, the consumer checks if the message ID has already been processed. After successful processing, the ID is added to the set and the message is ACKed. Also added a dead-letter stream for events that fail 3 times — routed to an admin dashboard for manual inspection.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Duplicate processing on consumer restartIdempotency check with processed-event set + ACK after processing
Poison messages block consumerRetry counter per message; move to dead-letter stream after 3 failures
Event schema changes break consumersVersion field in events; consumers handle v1 and v2 during migration
Consumer lag grows during peakScale consumers horizontally via consumer groups; monitor pending count

Validation Strategy

  • Exactly-once test: publish 100 events, crash consumer after 50, restart — verify exactly 100 notifications sent
  • Poison message test: publish malformed event, verify it moves to dead-letter after 3 retries
  • Schema evolution test: publish v1 and v2 events, verify consumer handles both
  • Lag test: pause consumer for 5 minutes, resume — verify all queued events processed in order

Definition

Architecting Event-Driven Design for 1M+ users means building a publish-subscribe system that handles millions of events per hour with guaranteed delivery, ordered processing within partitions, horizontal consumer scaling, and graceful degradation when brokers or consumers fail.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled HR Leave Management System for 1M+ employees across 500 departments, each leave action generates events consumed by 5 downstream services. At month-end: 100K leave submissions/hour → 500K events/hour (5 consumers each). Manoj would design partitioned Redis Streams by department, consumer groups per service, and a saga orchestrator for the approval workflow spanning leave, balance, calendar, and notification services.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Saga orchestrator for leave approval
interface SagaStep {
  name: string;
  execute: (context: any) => Promise;
  compensate: (context: any) => Promise;
}

class LeaveApprovalSaga {
  private steps: SagaStep[] = [
    {
      name: 'approve-leave',
      execute: async (ctx) => {
        await prisma.leave.update({ where: { id: ctx.leaveId }, data: { status: 'APPROVED' } });
        ctx.previousStatus = 'PENDING';
      },
      compensate: async (ctx) => {
        await prisma.leave.update({ where: { id: ctx.leaveId }, data: { status: ctx.previousStatus } });
      },
    },
    {
      name: 'debit-balance',
      execute: async (ctx) => {
        const result = await prisma.leaveBalance.update({
          where: { employeeId: ctx.employeeId },
          data: { remaining: { decrement: ctx.days } },
        });
        if (result.remaining < 0) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
      },
      compensate: async (ctx) => {
        await prisma.leaveBalance.update({
          where: { employeeId: ctx.employeeId },
          data: { remaining: { increment: ctx.days } },
        });
      },
    },
    {
      name: 'block-calendar',
      execute: async (ctx) => {
        await prisma.calendarBlock.create({
          data: { employeeId: ctx.employeeId, startDate: ctx.startDate, endDate: ctx.endDate, leaveId: ctx.leaveId },
        });
      },
      compensate: async (ctx) => {
        await prisma.calendarBlock.deleteMany({ where: { leaveId: ctx.leaveId } });
      },
    },
  ];

  async execute(context: any) {
    const completed: SagaStep[] = [];
    for (const step of this.steps) {
      try {
        await step.execute(context);
        completed.push(step);
        await redis.xAdd('saga-events', '*', {
          event: JSON.stringify({ type: `${step.name}-completed`, ...context }),
        });
      } catch (err) {
        console.error(`Saga step ${step.name} failed:`, err);
        // Compensate in reverse order
        for (const completedStep of completed.reverse()) {
          await completedStep.compensate(context);
          await redis.xAdd('saga-events', '*', {
            event: JSON.stringify({ type: `${completedStep.name}-compensated`, ...context }),
          });
        }
        throw new Error(`Saga failed at ${step.name}: ${err.message}`);
      }
    }
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After deploying the event-driven saga for leave approval, some employees had their leave approved but balance not decremented. Others had balance decremented but calendar not blocked — creating scheduling conflicts.

Investigation: Manoj found that the saga was running with eventual consistency but no orchestration. Each consumer independently processed events without coordination. When the balance-service was slow (high DB load), the calendar-service processed the event first, creating a calendar block before confirming sufficient balance.

Fix: Replaced the choreography pattern (each consumer listens independently) with a saga orchestrator that runs steps sequentially. If any step fails, compensating transactions run in reverse order. Added a saga state table in PostgreSQL to track progress and enable resume-after-crash. Result: zero inconsistencies in the next month-end cycle.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Choreography leads to inconsistent stateUse saga orchestrator for multi-step workflows
Compensating transaction failsLog compensation failures to dead-letter; manual reconciliation dashboard
Event ordering across partitionsPartition by department ID; order guaranteed within partition
Consumer lag under peak loadAuto-scale consumers based on pending message count

Validation Strategy

  • Saga integrity test: approve 1000 leaves concurrently, verify balance = initial - sum(approved days) for each employee
  • Compensation test: inject failure at step 2, verify all prior steps compensated and leave status reverted
  • Ordering test: submit 10 leaves for same employee rapidly, verify processed in submission order
  • Scale test: simulate 100K events/hour across 500 department partitions, verify consumer lag < 30s

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Microservices Boundary examines how to decompose a monolith into services: bounded context identification (DDD), data ownership, inter-service communication (sync vs. async), shared-nothing architecture, service granularity trade-offs, and strategies for splitting databases without downtime.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj would identify 4 bounded contexts: (1) Identity — authentication, roles, JWT lifecycle, (2) Leave Management — leave requests, approval chains, business rules, (3) Calendar — team schedules, holiday calendar, overlap detection, (4) Notifications — Socket.io events, email, in-app alerts. Each owns its data. The challenge: the approval workflow crosses Identity (who can approve?) and Leave Management (what's the leave status?).

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Anti-pattern: Cross-boundary database access
// Leave service directly queries auth database
const approver = await authDb.query(
  'SELECT role FROM users WHERE id = $1', [approverId]
);
if (approver.role !== 'MANAGER') throw new Error('Not authorized');
// This couples Leave service to Identity service's schema!

// Correct: Use JWT claims (data travels with the request)
app.post('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req) => {
  // Role is embedded in JWT by Identity service
  const { role, department } = req.user; // From JWT payload
  if (role !== 'MANAGER' && role !== 'HR_ADMIN') {
    return reply.code(403).send({ error: 'Only managers can approve leaves' });
  }

  // Leave service owns approval logic — no cross-boundary query
  const leave = await prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
  if (leave.departmentId !== department && role !== 'HR_ADMIN') {
    return reply.code(403).send({ error: 'Can only approve within your department' });
  }

  const updated = await prisma.leave.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { status: 'APPROVED', approvedBy: req.user.id, approvedAt: new Date() },
  });

  // Publish event — other services react asynchronously
  await redis.xAdd('leave-events', '*', {
    event: JSON.stringify({
      type: 'LeaveApproved',
      data: { leaveId: updated.id, employeeId: updated.employeeId, days: updated.days },
    }),
  });

  return updated;
});

// Strangler fig: Gradually extract Calendar from monolith
app.get('/api/calendar/team/:deptId', async (req) => {
  const useNewService = await featureFlag('calendar-service-enabled', req.params.deptId);
  if (useNewService) {
    // Route to new Calendar microservice
    return fetch(`${CALENDAR_SVC}/team/${req.params.deptId}`).then(r => r.json());
  }
  // Fall back to monolith's calendar logic
  return getTeamCalendarFromMonolith(req.params.deptId);
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After extracting the Calendar service from the monolith, team schedule queries started returning stale data. Employees saw available dates that were actually blocked by approved leaves.

Investigation: Manoj found the root cause: the Calendar service had its own database, but the Leave service was still writing calendar blocks to the old monolith database. The Calendar service was reading from cal_db which had no leave-related blocks. Data ownership was split but the write path wasn't updated.

Fix: (1) Made Leave service publish LeaveApproved events. (2) Calendar service subscribed and created blocks in cal_db. (3) Ran a one-time migration to backfill existing leave blocks into cal_db. (4) Added a reconciliation job that compared leave_db and cal_db daily, flagging mismatches. Result: calendar accuracy restored within 1 day.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Services share a database (hidden coupling)Each service owns its database; communicate via APIs/events
Distributed transactions across servicesSaga pattern with compensating actions instead of 2PC
Too-fine granularity (nano-services)Start with larger bounded contexts; split only when pain justifies it
Data duplication leads to inconsistencyEvent-driven sync + daily reconciliation job

Validation Strategy

  • Dependency test: deploy each service independently; verify no cross-service DB connections
  • Strangler fig: route 10% traffic to new Calendar service, compare results with monolith
  • Data consistency: reconciliation job reports zero mismatches for 7 consecutive days before full cutover
  • Failure isolation: take down Notification service, verify leave approval still works

Definition

Architecting Microservices Boundary for 1M+ users requires strategic service decomposition that balances team autonomy, independent scalability, and operational complexity. At this scale, each service must handle its proportional share of load independently, with clear data ownership, well-defined APIs, and circuit breakers to prevent cascade failures.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled CRM Sales Dashboard for 1M sales reps, Manoj would decompose the monolith into: (1) Pipeline Service (deal CRUD, stage transitions — 50% of all writes), (2) Analytics Service (aggregations, reports — read-heavy, cacheable), (3) Lead Service (multi-step lead forms, scoring), (4) User Service (authentication, preferences). Pipeline and Analytics scale independently: Pipeline needs write-optimized PostgreSQL, Analytics needs read-optimized MongoDB aggregation with materialized views.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Service boundary enforcement via API contracts
// Pipeline Service — owns deal data, exposes REST API
// shared/contracts/pipeline.ts (shared type package)
export interface Deal {
  id: string;
  title: string;
  value: number;
  stage: 'prospect' | 'qualified' | 'proposal' | 'negotiation' | 'closed_won' | 'closed_lost';
  ownerId: string;
  leadId: string;
  updatedAt: string;
}

export interface DealStageChangedEvent {
  type: 'DealStageChanged';
  data: { dealId: string; from: Deal['stage']; to: Deal['stage']; value: number; ownerId: string };
  timestamp: number;
}

// Pipeline Service implementation
app.patch('/api/deals/:id/stage', async (req, reply) => {
  const { stage } = req.body;
  const deal = await prisma.deal.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
  if (deal.ownerId !== req.user.id) return reply.code(403).send({ error: 'Not your deal' });

  const updated = await prisma.deal.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { stage, updatedAt: new Date() },
  });

  // Publish event for Analytics service (async, non-blocking)
  await redis.xAdd('deal-events', '*', {
    event: JSON.stringify({
      type: 'DealStageChanged',
      data: { dealId: updated.id, from: deal.stage, to: stage, value: deal.value, ownerId: deal.ownerId },
      timestamp: Date.now(),
    }),
  });

  return updated;
});

// Analytics Service — consumes events, maintains read-optimized views
async function consumeDealEvents() {
  try { await redis.xGroupCreate('deal-events', 'analytics-group', '0', { MKSTREAM: true }); } catch {}

  while (true) {
    const messages = await redis.xReadGroup('analytics-group', 'analytics-1', [
      { key: 'deal-events', id: '>' }
    ], { COUNT: 50, BLOCK: 5000 });

    if (!messages) continue;
    for (const msg of messages[0].messages) {
      const event = JSON.parse(msg.message.event);
      if (event.type === 'DealStageChanged') {
        await mongo.collection('pipeline_summary').updateOne(
          { ownerId: event.data.ownerId, stage: event.data.to },
          { $inc: { count: 1, totalValue: event.data.value } },
          { upsert: true },
        );
        if (event.data.from) {
          await mongo.collection('pipeline_summary').updateOne(
            { ownerId: event.data.ownerId, stage: event.data.from },
            { $inc: { count: -1, totalValue: -event.data.value } },
          );
        }
      }
      await redis.xAck('deal-events', 'analytics-group', msg.id);
    }
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After splitting Analytics into its own service, the sales pipeline dashboard showed incorrect totals. Some deals appeared counted in two stages simultaneously.

Investigation: Manoj traced the event flow. The Analytics consumer was processing DealStageChanged events but during a consumer restart, some events were replayed (at-least-once delivery). The consumer incremented the new stage count but didn't check if the decrement of the old stage had already been applied.

Fix: Added idempotency: each event's stream ID was tracked in a processed_events collection. Before processing, the consumer checks if the event ID exists. Also added a nightly reconciliation job that re-aggregates from the Pipeline service's API and compares with the Analytics materialized view, auto-correcting any drift.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Event replay causes double-countingIdempotency tracking per event ID in consumer
Service boundaries too fine (10+ services for small team)Conway's Law: one service per team (2-pizza rule)
Cross-service queries become complexCQRS: write to source, read from pre-built views
Deployment complexity grows with service countShared CI/CD templates, Docker Compose for local dev

Validation Strategy

  • Independence test: deploy Pipeline service without Analytics — verify deal CRUD works
  • Consistency test: change 1000 deal stages, compare Pipeline DB totals with Analytics materialized view
  • Scale test: auto-scale Analytics to 5 replicas under load, verify consumer group distributes events evenly
  • Contract test: use Pact to verify Pipeline API contract hasn't broken Analytics consumer
